items.q: Most movements in the human body use which class of lever?
items.why: Students assume the most familiar (first class - seesaw) is most common. Actually most body levers are third class (effort between fulcrum and load) - like the biceps curl. They sacrifice mechanical advantage for speed/range.

items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.q: What is the difference between strength and power?
items.correct: Strength is maximum force; power is force times speed of movement.
items.random_wrong: Strength is for the upper body, power for the lower.
items.why: Students conflate strength and power. A strong person can lift heavy slowly; a powerful athlete generates force QUICKLY (sprinter, shot-putter). Power requires both force and velocity.

items.q: A biceps curl is an example of:
items.why: It's Third-class lever. “First-class lever” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Mechanical advantage > 1 means the lever amplifies:
items.why: Correct answer: Force. “Speed” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If effort arm is 0.5 m and load arm is 0.1 m, mechanical advantage is?
items.why: The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
